question: Define the interior of a set A in a metric space (X, d). Explain the concept using the idea of an open ball.
expectedPoints: The interior of A, denoted int(A) or A°, is the union of all open subsets contained within A.
A point x is an interior point of A if there exists some ε > 0 such that the open ball B(x; ε) is completely contained in A.
The interior of A is the largest open set contained in A.

question: Prove that the interior of any set A in a metric space is an open set.
expectedPoints: Let x be an arbitrary point in int(A). By definition, there exists an open ball B(x; ε) contained in int(A).
Since B(x; ε) is open and contained in A, every point y in B(x; ε) is also an interior point of A.
Therefore, for each y in B(x; ε), there exists a δ > 0 such that B(y; δ) is contained in A, making B(x; ε) a subset of int(A).
This shows that int(A) contains an open ball around each of its points, fulfilling the definition of an open set.

question: Let Q be the set of rational numbers with the standard Euclidean metric. Determine the interior of Q and justify your answer thoroughly, connecting your reasoning to the fundamental properties of the rational and irrational numbers.
expectedPoints: The interior of Q is the empty set, int(Q) = ∅.
Justification: For any rational point q and any ε > 0, the open interval (q - ε, q + ε) must contain irrational numbers.
Therefore, no open ball B(q; ε) is entirely contained within Q, as it will always contain points outside of Q (the irrationals).
Since no point in Q is an interior point, the union of all open sets contained in Q is empty.
This result relies on the key property that both the rationals and irrationals are dense in the real numbers.
